title: Du Fu
summary: Du Fu (712–770) is a poet of the Chinese Tang dynasty, revered for centuries as the shi sheng, the 'poet-sage': the moral realist whose verse turned the ruin of the An Lushan Rebellion into poetic witness, detail that complements but does not replace the historical record of a society coming apart, and who, respected in limited circles but not yet accorded his later canonical stature, was in time crowned perhaps the greatest poet in the Chinese tradition.
hook: He failed the imperial examinations, drifted through a collapsing empire as a minor official and a refugee, and once came home from the capital to find his small son had starved to death. He died sick and poor on a boat on a southern river, and almost no one in his own century thought his poems remarkable. Three hundred years later, Chinese critics had settled on a single word for him: sage, the same word reserved for Confucius. A thousand years after that, schoolchildren still know his lines by heart.
associatedWorks: The Ballad of the Army Carts
My Thatched Cottage Unroofed by the Autumn Wind

beginner.theFigure:
For a few quiet years, Du Fu lived in a hut with a grass roof beside a creek near Chengdu, in western China. He planted vegetables, watched herons on the water, and wrote poems about his neighbors, his wife, and his children. These are some of the gentlest poems in the Chinese language.

But peace was rare in his life. Du Fu was born in the year 712, when the Tang dynasty seemed rich and strong. He trained for years to become a government official, like others in his family before him. He wanted a steady post so he could serve the country and feed his family. He took the great state exam. He failed it. Good jobs never came, and he spent much of his life poor and on the move.

Then, when Du Fu was in his forties, a terrible war broke out. A powerful general named An Lushan turned against the emperor. Cities burned, armies marched, and millions of people died or fled their homes. Du Fu lived through the whole disaster. He wrote down what he saw. That war, and the ruin it left, became the great subject of his poetry.
beginner.bodyOfWork:
Du Fu wrote about ordinary suffering. He wrote about young men dragged off to fight, about mothers left with no sons, about tax collectors pounding on doors at night. He noticed the pain that other poets stepped around, and he put it into plain, exact words.

He also wrote about small, quiet things. A spring morning. An old friend visiting after many years. The moon over a strange town where he could not sleep. He looked at the world closely, and he made his readers look too.

Du Fu was a master of a strict poetry form called regulated verse. It had firm rules about length, sound, and matching lines. Most poets found the rules stiff. Du Fu made them sing. Inside those tight limits he packed huge feeling and sharp images, so that a short poem could hold a whole broken world.

Over the years, about fifteen hundred of his poems were saved and passed down. Together they read almost like a diary of a man and his times.
beginner.howBeenRead:
For a long time after his death, few people thought Du Fu was special. He died far from home, worn down by illness and want, while still on the road through the southern provinces. In his own century, other poets were more famous.

Then, slowly, readers began to see how great he was. A few hundred years later, under the Song dynasty, scholars decided he was the finest poet China had ever produced. They gave him a rare title: the poet-sage. The word sage was the same one used for Confucius, the ancient teacher. It was the highest praise they could give.

They also called his work poetry that is history. His poems record real events of the war so clearly that people read them almost like a record of the age. To this day, Chinese schoolchildren learn his lines by heart. One of his most loved poems begins with a broken city where the grass still grows deep in spring.
beginner.whyItEndured:
Why has Du Fu lasted for more than a thousand years? One reason is honesty. He did not hide from pain. He wrote about hunger, fear, and loss in plain, careful words. People who have suffered still find their own lives in his poems.

Another reason is his sense of duty. Du Fu was shaped by Confucian ideas, which taught that an educated person should care about the state and about ordinary people. Even when he was poor and powerless, he worried about the country and the farmers around him. His poems carry that steady, caring gaze.

A third reason is skill. Du Fu could do things with the Chinese language that few others could match. Later poets studied him the way music students study a great composer. He became a teacher to every poet who came after him, whether they copied him or pushed against him.

There is also his friend Li Bai. Li Bai was the other giant of Tang poetry, wild and joyful where Du Fu was serious and sad. The two men knew each other and admired each other. Chinese readers have loved pairing them ever since, like two halves of one golden age.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Today Du Fu belongs to everyone who reads Chinese, and even to many who do not. But how to see him is not always easy.

In modern China, he is often held up as a poet of the people, a man who felt the pain of the poor. There is real truth in this. Yet Du Fu was also a loyal servant of the empire who longed for an official post his whole life. Turning him only into a friend of the common people can leave that part out.

His fame has also spread far beyond China. His poems have been put into many languages, and a British film once called him China's greatest poet, setting him beside Shakespeare and Dante. Some readers worry that in translation his careful art gets lost, since so much of his power lives in the exact sound and shape of the Chinese.

Even his birthday has become a kind of playground. In 2012, on the 1,300th year after his birth, Chinese students drew funny cartoons over his textbook portrait and shared them online. Some found it rude. Others said it showed he was still alive in people's minds, close enough to joke about.

general.theFigure:
In 755 a trusted frontier general named An Lushan turned his armies against the Tang throne, and an empire that had seemed permanent began to break apart. Du Fu was then in his forties, a talented man who had never found the official career he wanted, and the catastrophe made him into the poet later ages would revere. He had trained since boyhood for the civil service, sitting the imperial examinations and failing them, drifting between minor posts and long stretches of poverty. When the rebellion came, it swept him up along with everyone else. He was captured by rebels, escaped, served briefly at the exiled court, lost that position, and spent his last years wandering the southwest with his family, always short of money and often ill. Much of this itinerary, along with the offices he held and their dates, is reconstructed from his own poems rather than from independent records.

What raised this unlucky life into greatness was the way Du Fu watched. He treated his own hardships and the ruin around him as things worth recording exactly. Where earlier poets reached for graceful feeling, he reached for the specific fact: the conscript's fear, the empty granary, the price of grain, the knock of an official after dark. He folded public disaster and private grief into the same lines, so that the fall of a dynasty and the death of his own child could sit within a single poem. That fusion of the historical and the personal is the core of what he made.
general.bodyOfWork:
Roughly fifteen hundred of Du Fu's poems survive, and their range is wide. There are tender domestic scenes from his years in a thatched cottage near Chengdu, where for a time he found rest, kept a garden, and wrote about his wife and children with unusual warmth. There are travel poems, drinking poems, and poems to distant friends. And there are the great poems of witness, such as the sequence known as the Three Officers and Three Partings, which follows ordinary families as the army tears their sons away.

Du Fu's technical command set him apart. He is the acknowledged master of regulated verse, a demanding form with fixed length, strict tonal patterns, and required parallel couplets. In lesser hands the rules produced stiff, decorative poems. Du Fu used them to compress enormous pressure into a few lines, balancing image against image so that the form itself seemed to strain under the weight of feeling. Later critics gave his poetry a second name beyond poet-sage. They called it shi shi, poetry that is history, because his verse recorded the events and mood of his age with a fidelity that ordinary chronicles could not match.
general.howBeenRead:
Du Fu's standing was not obvious to those who knew him. He died in 770 far to the south, worn out by illness and hardship, and the anthologies of his own century barely noticed him. His rise came later. During the Song dynasty, two and three centuries on, scholars and poets settled on him as the supreme master, the model against whom all others were measured. They honored him as the shi sheng, the poet-sage, borrowing the term of highest reverence, the same word attached to Confucius.

That judgment shaped how China read poetry for the next thousand years. To be a serious poet was, in part, to have an argument with Du Fu. His careful craft, his moral seriousness, and his blending of the personal and the political became a standard that later writers absorbed or resisted, but could not ignore. His reputation also carried a particular idea of what poetry was for. If Du Fu was the greatest, then poetry was not mainly play or ornament. It was a way of bearing honest witness to one's time, and of taking responsibility for the suffering one observed.
general.whyItEndured:
Part of Du Fu's endurance rests on the plain human truth of what he described. Hunger, displacement, the dread of conscription, the ache of separation from family: these belong to no single century, and readers who have known them keep finding themselves in his lines. His refusal to look away, and his exactness about detail, give the poems a weight that outlasts their occasion.

A second source is the Confucian conscience running through the work. Du Fu had absorbed the idea that an educated man owed loyalty to the state and care to the common people, and he held to it even when the state had no use for him. His poems worry about farmers, soldiers, and the fate of the dynasty rather than retreating into private calm. That posture, of the powerless scholar who still feels responsible for the whole society, gave later readers a moral image of the poet they could admire and imitate.

He is also inseparable from his friend and near-contemporary Li Bai. Li Bai was the romantic, a self-styled wanderer drawn to wine and the Daoist love of nature and freedom, where Du Fu was the sober moral realist. The two men met, traveled together, and wrote to each other, and Chinese tradition has paired them ever since as the twin peaks of Tang poetry. Holding them together, readers get two ways of being a poet, and an age large enough to contain both.
general.contestedInheritance:
Because Du Fu means so much, later ages have kept remaking him in their own image. Modern China has often cast him as a poet of the people, a voice for the poor and the exploited, and there is genuine ground for this in his sympathy for suffering farmers and soldiers. Yet the same poems show a man who yearned for high office, who defined himself by loyalty to the throne, and who grieved the disorder of the empire as much as the misery of the poor. A reading that keeps only the populist Du Fu quietly drops the loyal Confucian official he also was.

His global travels raise a different problem. Du Fu has been translated into many languages, and a 2020 British documentary presented him to Western audiences as China's greatest poet, ranking him with Shakespeare and Dante. The praise is sincere, but so is the difficulty. More than most poets, Du Fu lives in the exact sound, compression, and parallel structure of classical Chinese, and translations tend to carry his subjects while losing the craft that made him supreme at home. The Du Fu admired abroad and the Du Fu revered in China are not quite the same figure. Bridging that gap, without flattening either version, is the ongoing task of anyone who wants to carry him across languages.

advanced.theFigure:
The received portrait of Du Fu turns on a familiar arc: the frustrated office-seeker whom the An Lushan Rebellion transformed into the conscience of his age. The arc is not false, but it flattens a harder question that recent scholarship has pressed, namely how much of the Du Fu we revere is a construction of later readers rather than a recovery of the eighth-century man. Almost nothing in his own century marked him as exceptional; his canonization was the work of Song critics who found in him a model for their own preoccupations with moral seriousness and formal control. The figure of the poet-sage is thus partly retrospective, assembled from a corpus read backward through the values of the readers who elevated it.

This matters because it destabilizes the intuitive move from the poems to the man. Du Fu's first-person voice is so circumstantial, so saturated with dates, prices, and named places, that it invites the reader to treat the poetry as transparent autobiography. Eva Shan Chou and others have cautioned against exactly this. The self that speaks in the poems is a crafted persona, shaped by convention and by Du Fu's own sense of how a witness should sound, and the temptation to read the anguish as unmediated report is the same temptation that later produced the sentimental poet of the people. The discipline the poems demand is to hold their documentary texture and their artifice together, rather than dissolving one into the other.
advanced.bodyOfWork:
The reverent label that treats Du Fu's verse as a form of chronicle is more double-edged than its admiring use suggests. It credits him with a fidelity to event that ordinary record-keeping lacks, but it also imports a claim about what his poems are doing, treating lyric compression as though it were testimony. The most demanding poems resist that reduction. The long sequence written on the road to Fengxian, and the paired poems that follow conscripted families through the war, do not merely tally the rebellion's cost; they stage the poet's own compromised position as a minor official who eats while others starve, and they turn that guilt into structure. To read them as reportage is to miss that their subject is partly the ethics of witnessing itself.

His formal achievement raises a related difficulty that is easy to assert and hard to convey. Du Fu's supremacy in regulated verse rests on effects that are nearly untranslatable, because they inhere in properties of classical Chinese that other languages do not share: tonal counterpoint, the semantic torque of parallel couplets, the way a strict frame is made to buckle under emotional load. This is not a sentimental point about lost beauty. It has a sharp consequence. The qualities that earned Du Fu his rank at home are precisely the ones that vanish in translation, so that the global reader admires him for his subjects while the Chinese tradition reveres him for a craft the global reader cannot see. The two audiences are, in a real sense, praising different poets.
advanced.howBeenRead:
The elevation of Du Fu in the Song was not a neutral recognition of merit but an act with stakes. The critics who made him supreme were building a vision of the poet as moral agent, and Du Fu, with his Confucian conscience and his refusal of easy consolation, served that vision as Li Bai could not. The pairing of the two, so beloved in the tradition, is itself an argument disguised as a friendship. It sets the disciplined, responsible, earthbound poet against the spontaneous, Daoist-inflected genius, and the tradition's steady preference for Du Fu encodes a judgment about what poetry is for. To canonize Du Fu as the sage was to say that the highest poetry answers to duty and history rather than to inspiration and escape.

The uncomfortable implication is that Du Fu's greatness and his usefulness are difficult to separate. Every age that has claimed him has needed him for something: the Song for a model of engaged craft, the modern Chinese state for a poet of the masses, the contemporary West for a humane universal voice to set beside its own canon. None of these readings is simply wrong, and each rests on something genuinely in the poems. But their accumulation should make a careful reader wary. A figure so serviceable to so many programs is one whose reception has to be read as skeptically as his verse, because the real Du Fu is always at risk of being the one a given interpreter requires.
advanced.whyItEndured:
Accounts of Du Fu's endurance usually reach for his universality, the claim that hunger, war, and loss are timeless and that his honesty about them travels. The claim is true enough to be worth distrusting. It was precisely by abstracting his subjects from their craft that translation made him a world poet, and the universalizing reading purchases its reach by quietly conceding the loss of the formal genius on which his domestic reputation actually rests. Endurance and translatability are not the same virtue, and Du Fu's case pulls them apart: what endures globally is a subset of what made him great locally.

The more precise account of his survival is institutional. Du Fu endured because the Song critics built him into the foundation of a tradition, making fluency in his work a condition of literary seriousness for the thousand years that followed. Poets learned him not because his pain was universal but because the canon required it; his lines became the shared idiom against which ambition measured itself. This is a less flattering explanation than timeless human truth, but it is sturdier, and it accounts for something the universalist story cannot: why a poet largely ignored in his own lifetime became, and remained, inescapable at home long before the wider world had heard of him. The durability lived first in the institution of the canon, and only much later in the sentiment of readers who found their own suffering in his.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The sharpest quarrel over Du Fu is not between China and the West but within the Chinese reception, and it concerns which Du Fu the tradition is entitled to keep. The populist reading, dominant since the twentieth century, casts Du Fu as a champion of the laboring masses, and it can point to real poems of scalding sympathy for the conscript and the tax-crushed farmer. But it must suppress the Du Fu who defined his life by the pursuit of office, who mourned the rebellion as a wound to the legitimate order, and whose sympathy for the poor is inseparable from a hierarchical Confucian ideal of the official's paternal care. Stripped of that frame, his compassion is modernized into something it was not, and the loyalist becomes a proto-democrat by editorial subtraction.

The most defensible position refuses both the sentimental populist and the merely aesthetic Du Fu, and it is uncomfortable because it denies the reader the consolation each offers. Take the moment, endlessly quoted, when he wishes for a great mansion of a thousand rooms to shelter all the cold scholars of the world, while his own roof blows away in an autumn gale. The populist hears a cry for the masses; the formalist hears a virtuoso managing a difficult poem. Both are reading half of it. The lines are at once a real ethical impulse and a self-conscious performance of the Confucian scholar's proper feeling, and they draw their power from holding those together rather than resolving into either. To honor Du Fu is to resist the urge to make him simpler than the poems, which are built precisely on the tension the interpreters keep trying to dissolve.

foundation.theStory: Du Fu poses the problem of how a life of failure becomes, centuries later, the summit of a tradition. He was born in 712 into a scholar-official family, trained for the civil service, and failed to secure the career he wanted. The An Lushan Rebellion that erupted in 755 shattered the Tang world and drove him into years of wandering, capture, and poverty; he died in 770, still largely unrecognized. Yet by the Song dynasty he had been crowned the poet-sage, the greatest poet in Chinese history. He is the case study for watching a reputation being made after the fact, and for asking what in the poems earned it.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates are about interpretation and ownership. One concerns how far to trust the poems as autobiography: Du Fu's verse is so circumstantial that it invites reading as direct reportage, yet the speaking self is a crafted persona, and scholars such as Eva Shan Chou warn against collapsing art into confession. A second concerns which Du Fu is authentic. Modern China often presents him as a poet of the people, while the poems also show a man who longed for imperial office and grieved the dynasty's disorder. A third concerns translation: whether a poet whose greatness lies in the sound and structure of classical Chinese can cross into other languages without becoming a different writer.
foundation.whyItMatters: Working through Du Fu opens the Tang dynasty at its height and its collapse, the Confucian ideal of the scholar-official, the technical world of classical Chinese poetry, and the long history of how canons are built. It connects to broad questions about the relationship between suffering and art, about whether poetry can serve as historical testimony, and about what happens to a national treasure when it is carried across languages and enlisted by later politics.
questions: Du Fu was largely ignored in his lifetime and crowned the greatest poet centuries later. What does it take for a reputation to be made after death, and who gets to decide?
Du Fu wrote his own grief, including the death of his son, into poems about a national disaster. Does knowing that the events were real change how you read the poems, and should it?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The modern English-language study of Du Fu begins with William Hung's Tu Fu: China's Greatest Poet (1952), the first full biography to weave the life through the poems. Stephen Owen has shaped the field on two fronts, as a historian of High Tang poetry and as the translator of the complete poems, an enormous scholarly edition that made the whole corpus available in English. Eva Shan Chou's Reconsidering Tu Fu pressed the argument that the poems' apparent transparency is itself a construction, and that Du Fu's greatness is bound up with how later ages read him. David Hawkes's close teaching of individual poems remains a standard way in. The fault lines run between reading Du Fu as witness and reading him as artificer, and between the man of the eighth century and the sage assembled by his Song admirers.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is unusually rich on one side and thin on the other. Roughly fifteen hundred poems survive, well preserved and closely studied, giving an intimate record of a mind and its times. What is scarce is independent external testimony about Du Fu's life. Much of what is known is inferred from the poems themselves, which is a circular foundation: the verse is read as biography, and the biography is then used to interpret the verse. Dates, places, and public events can often be corroborated against Tang records, but the inner life, the motives, and the precise circumstances of many poems rest on the poet's own crafted account. The honest limit is that Du Fu's poetry is the main witness to Du Fu, and it is not a neutral one.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong position has to decline the two easy exits. It cannot treat the poems as pure documentary, because their speaker is a made thing and their power lies partly in artifice; and it cannot treat them as pure craft, because their moral weight is real and central to why they endured. The defensible reading holds both, and it can be shown on a single poem. When Du Fu, his own roof torn off by an autumn storm, wishes for a vast mansion to shelter all the poor scholars of the world, the lines are neither simple compassion nor mere technical display. They are a Confucian scholar performing, and genuinely feeling, the responsibility his tradition assigns him, and their force comes from refusing to separate the ethical impulse from the self-aware art. A good argument about Du Fu shows how a specific poem sustains that tension rather than choosing a side.
questions: Most of what we know about Du Fu's life comes from his own poems. What kinds of claims can such a source reliably support, and where must a careful reader stop trusting it?
If Du Fu's greatness lies largely in features of classical Chinese that do not survive translation, in what sense is the poet admired in English the same poet revered in China?

research.topic: Du Fu's life, corpus, and posthumous canonization
research.content: It is well established that Du Fu was born in 712 into a family of scholar-officials near Luoyang, that he trained for the imperial civil service and never achieved the career he sought, and that the An Lushan Rebellion, which began in 755, devastated the Tang empire and dominated the second half of his life. It is equally well established that he wrote in classical Chinese, was a master of regulated verse, died in 770 while traveling in the south, and was largely unrecognized in his own time before being canonized under the Song dynasty as the shi sheng, the poet-sage. Roughly fifteen hundred of his poems survive. These outlines rest on his own works and a long, consistent scholarly tradition.

research.topic: Du Fu's reputation and persona as objects of study
research.content: A significant line of recent scholarship treats Du Fu's reputation itself as the object of study, rather than taking his greatness as a given to be described. This work examines how Song-dynasty critics constructed the poet-sage, how the first-person voice of the poems functions as a crafted persona rather than a transparent record, and how successive ages, including the modern Chinese state and Western translators, have remade him to suit their needs. Associated with scholars including Eva Shan Chou and Stephen Owen, it shifts attention from the man to the long history of his reception, and it remains an active rather than settled area of interpretation.

research.topic: The lived reality behind the poems
research.content: What cannot be securely recovered is the lived reality behind many individual poems and the inner life they claim to report. Because independent records of Du Fu's daily circumstances are scarce, most of what is said about his motives, his relationships, and the exact occasions of his poems is inferred from the poems themselves, which are shaped by convention and craft. Resolving this would require external, contemporaneous testimony that does not survive. What can be responsibly inferred is that the public events his poems reference, the rebellion, the displacements, the offices he held, are largely corroborated by Tang records. What must remain genuinely uncertain is how closely the feeling and self-portrait in the verse match the private man, and how much of the received biography is history rather than a reading of the poetry.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Poet-Sage
comparativeNarrative.content: In the mainstream Chinese tradition, Du Fu is the poet-sage, the summit against which all others are measured. This view was largely built after his death, when Song-dynasty critics found in him a model of moral seriousness joined to supreme technical control. They prized his mastery of regulated verse and his refusal to separate private feeling from public duty. They gave his work a second name, poetry that is history, because it recorded the age with rare exactness. In this reading, Du Fu defines what poetry is for: honest witness and responsibility rather than ornament or escape. To be a serious Chinese poet ever after meant, in part, learning from him and answering to his example.

comparativeNarrative.name: Poet of the People
comparativeNarrative.content: Modern China has often read Du Fu as a voice for the common people. His poems about conscripted soldiers, ruined farmers, and families torn apart by war are taught in schools as proof of his sympathy for the poor. There is real ground for this. Du Fu did look hard at suffering that other poets ignored, and he wrote it plainly. Yet this framing can quietly set aside another Du Fu the poems also show: a man who longed all his life for high office and who grieved the collapse of the empire as a wound to its rightful order. The people's poet was also a loyal official, and reading him only as the first leaves out the second.

comparativeNarrative.name: China's Shakespeare
comparativeNarrative.content: In the West, Du Fu arrives as China's greatest poet, a figure to set beside Shakespeare and Dante, as a 2020 British documentary did. Translated into many languages, he reaches readers who value his humane attention to war, loss, and ordinary life. But this Du Fu is shaped by what translation can carry. His standing at home rests heavily on the sound, compression, and paired structure of classical Chinese, and those effects rarely survive the move into another language. Foreign readers tend to meet his subjects while missing the craft that earned his rank. The admiration is genuine, yet the poet celebrated abroad and the master revered in China are not quite the same person.

comparativeSummary: Held together, these vantage points show a poet whose greatness is settled but whose meaning keeps being remade. The classical tradition crowned him the sage of moral craft; modern China recast him as a friend of the poor; the West receives him as a universal humane voice. Each reading rests on something real in the poems, and each quietly leaves something out, whether the loyal official, the demanding artist, or the untranslatable form. The disagreement is not about whether Du Fu matters but about which Du Fu a given age needs, and how much of the whole man survives being enlisted for a cause.

rabbitHole.label: The Tang Dynasty
rabbitHole.reason: Du Fu lived through the Tang at its cultural height and its near-collapse, and his poetry is unintelligible without that world. The dynasty's prosperity, its examination system, and its wars set the stage on which his life and verse unfolded.

rabbitHole.label: The An Lushan Rebellion
rabbitHole.reason: The rebellion that began in 755 devastated the Tang and drove Du Fu into wandering and poverty. It became the central subject of his mature poetry, which records the war's human cost with a fidelity that earned his verse the name poetry that is history.

rabbitHole.label: Li Bai
rabbitHole.reason: Li Bai was Du Fu's friend and the other giant of Tang poetry, the romantic and Daoist-inflected genius against whom Du Fu the moral realist is perpetually paired. The two are read together as the twin peaks of the age, each defining the other by contrast.

rabbitHole.label: Confucianism
rabbitHole.reason: Du Fu's sense that the educated man owes loyalty to the state and care to the people is a Confucian inheritance, and it shapes the moral seriousness later critics prized. His poems perform the scholar-official's conscience even when he held no office.

rabbitHole.label: Daoism
rabbitHole.reason: The Daoist love of nature, spontaneity, and escape animates Li Bai's poetry and defines by contrast Du Fu's earthbound, duty-driven realism. Setting the two worldviews side by side sharpens what is distinctive in Du Fu's engagement with history and society.

rabbitHole.label: The Song Dynasty
rabbitHole.reason: It was Song-dynasty critics and poets, centuries after his death, who crowned Du Fu the poet-sage and made him the model for serious poetry. His canonization is a product of Song literary values as much as of the Tang life he actually lived.

rabbitHole.label: Confucius
rabbitHole.reason: The honorific sage that Chinese critics gave Du Fu is the same reverent term attached to Confucius, and the borrowing signals the moral, not merely artistic, standing they assigned him. The link marks how poetry could be raised to the dignity of ethical teaching.

rabbitHole.label: Murasaki Shikibu
rabbitHole.reason: Like Du Fu, Murasaki is an East Asian literary figure whose towering reputation was consolidated by later readers and whose art is deeply bound to a language and court world that resist translation. The pairing illuminates how canons form around a founding voice.

reference.author: Owen, Stephen (trans.)
reference.title: The Poetry of Du Fu
reference.annotation: The first complete scholarly translation of Du Fu's roughly fifteen hundred poems into English, with apparatus. The entry relies on it as the fullest access in English to the whole corpus, and as the ground for claims about the range and difficulty of the work.

reference.author: Chou, Eva Shan
reference.title: Reconsidering Tu Fu: Literary Greatness and Cultural Context
reference.annotation: The study that reframed Du Fu's greatness as partly a matter of how later ages read him, and that warns against treating his circumstantial verse as transparent autobiography. The entry draws on it for the argument about persona and constructed reputation.

reference.author: Hung, William
reference.title: Tu Fu: China's Greatest Poet
reference.annotation: The first full English-language biography to read Du Fu's life through his poems, and still the fullest reconstruction of his movements year by year. The entry uses it for the narrative shape of the life and its close pairing with the rebellion that transformed his work.

reference.author: Hawkes, David
reference.title: A Little Primer of Tu Fu
reference.annotation: A close reading of thirty-five poems with the Chinese text, literal glosses, and commentary. The entry treats it as the demonstration of how Du Fu's regulated verse actually works line by line, and of why so much of that work is bound to the original language.

reference.author: Owen, Stephen
reference.title: The Great Age of Chinese Poetry: The High T'ang
reference.annotation: A study of the High Tang poetic world that places Du Fu among his contemporaries, including Li Bai and Wang Wei. The entry uses it to situate Du Fu within the age rather than in isolation, and to weigh his contemporary standing against his later fame.

commerce.author: Watson, Burton (trans.)
commerce.title: The Selected Poems of Du Fu
commerce.note: Come here first if you have never read Du Fu. Watson's clear, unfussy versions of a well-chosen hundred-odd poems let a newcomer feel the shape of his career, from the quiet Chengdu years to the poems of war, without needing any background in Chinese literature.

commerce.author: Young, David (trans.)
commerce.title: Du Fu: A Life in Poetry
commerce.note: Good for a reader who wants the life and the work braided together. Young arranges his translations to follow Du Fu's biography, so the poems arrive as moments in a story, which is the gentlest way to see how the events shaped the verse.

commerce.author: Hawkes, David
commerce.title: A Little Primer of Tu Fu
commerce.note: Turn to this once the translations make you wonder how the poems work in Chinese. Hawkes prints the original beside literal glosses and commentary for thirty-five poems, and it is the clearest demonstration of the craft that plain English versions cannot carry.

commerce.author: Owen, Stephen
commerce.title: The Great Age of Chinese Poetry: The High T'ang
commerce.note: Read this to see Du Fu among his rivals rather than alone on a pedestal. Owen sets him beside Li Bai, Wang Wei, and the wider Tang world, which is the context that makes his later canonization legible instead of inevitable.

commerce.author: Chou, Eva Shan
commerce.title: Reconsidering Tu Fu: Literary Greatness and Cultural Context
commerce.note: For the reader ready to argue about Du Fu rather than admire him. Chou treats his greatness as something built by later ages and reads the poems' seeming honesty as itself an achievement, which is where serious debate about him now starts.

commerce.author: Owen, Stephen (trans.)
commerce.title: The Poetry of Du Fu
commerce.note: The one to reach for when selections stop being enough. This complete six-volume translation gives every surviving poem with notes, so you can follow a theme across the whole corpus or check what an anthology quietly left out.

popularCulture.title: Du Fu: China's Greatest Poet (2020, Michael Wood / BBC)
popularCulture.description: This hour-long film follows the historian Michael Wood through the places Du Fu lived, while Ian McKellen reads the poems in English. It brings Du Fu to Western viewers and ranks him with Shakespeare and Dante. The warmth is real, though the grand comparison and the actor's voice can make a very Chinese poet sound like a familiar English one.

popularCulture.title: Spring View ('Spring Prospect', by Du Fu)
popularCulture.description: Written after rebels seized the capital, this short poem is one of the most memorized in China. It opens with a broken city where grass still grows deep in spring. In a few lines it holds a whole ruined country and one worried man. School versions can turn it into a neat patriotic lesson and hide the raw grief inside it.

popularCulture.title: Du Fu Thatched Cottage, Chengdu
popularCulture.description: In Chengdu, a park marks the spot where Du Fu once lived in a simple hut by a creek. A temple to him was built there long ago, and today gardens and halls draw many visitors. The calm, pretty grounds honor his memory, but they can make his years there look more peaceful than they were, since he was often poor, sick, and far from home.

popularCulture.title: The 'Du Fu Is Busy' meme (2012)
popularCulture.description: In 2012, on the 1,300th year after his birth, Chinese students drew cartoons over the textbook picture of Du Fu. They made him a soldier, a biker, and a delivery man, and shared the images online, where they went viral. Some people felt it mocked a national treasure. Others said it proved he still lived in young minds, close enough to play with.

popularCulture.title: Du Fu in the Chinese school curriculum
popularCulture.description: Nearly every child in China meets Du Fu at school and learns some of his lines by heart. This keeps him alive across a thousand years and ties him to national pride. But the classroom often trims him to a few famous, uplifting lines. The wandering, failing, grieving poet behind them can fade, leaving a smooth statue where a restless man once stood.
